title: U.S. seeks new coalition to reopen Strait of Hormuz to shipping amid Iranian-US blockades

contents:
# U.S. seeks new coalition to reopen Strait of Hormuz to shipping amid Iranian-US blockades

## Overview
The United States remains forming a new international coalition to restore commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz after Iran and the U.S. separately imposed blockades that have sharply reduced vessel transits. At least six ships crossed the strait in the previous 24 hours—far below normal traffic—while the U.S. and Iran remain deadlocked over conditions for reopening the waterway.

## Details
The Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil transit chokepoint, has become a major flashpoint as both Iran and the United States enforce maritime blockades, effectively halting most commercial traffic. Shipping data cited by Reuters show only a fraction of the usual vessel movements through the strait over the past day, underscoring the scale of disruption. The U.S. administration, after previously declaring the strait “COMPLETELY OPEN AND READY FOR BUSINESS,” remains now working with other countries to build a coalition that would underwrite and secure passage for commercial ships. Maritime experts note that the dual blockade has turned the Hormuz corridor into a global pressure point, with cascading impacts on energy markets and supply chains. The situation adds to broader regional tensions that also involve the Red Sea, where earlier Houthi attacks had already strained global shipping routes.

## Location
Strait of Hormuz, Middle East

## Source Context
Reporting attributed to coverage tracked under @IMOHQ (International Maritime Organization). Primary citation provides authoritative public reporting on this development. Cross-source verification through Perplexity sonar-indexed news outlets reinforces the account.
